candidate_problem: Intractability of the Branching Schrödinger Bridge (BSB) semi-coupling and RUOT relaxation
evidence_spans: A limitation of USB is that the semi-coupling of BSB and its RUOT relaxation are intractable. Consequently, we use WFR for approximation. The underlying mathematics merits further investigation.
problem_evidence_spans: A limitation of USB is that the semi-coupling of BSB and its RUOT relaxation are intractable.
feasibility_evidence_spans: We validated the effectiveness and robustness of the method on both synthetic and real scRNA-seq datasets
Algorithm 2 USB training workflow
auto_research_experiment: Develop a neural approximation of the BSB semi-coupling using a variational objective that minimizes the KL divergence between the WFR-based path and the true branching process, then compare against the current USB approximation.
available_data_or_benchmark: scRNA-seq datasets (e.g., Waddington-OT datasets or Schiebinger et al. 2019)
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/broadinstitute/wot
scientific_hypothesis: A learned neural semi-coupling for BSB will yield more accurate discrete cell-count predictions than the WFR approximation in systems with high proliferation rates.
expected_metric: Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) between predicted and ground-truth cell distributions at snapshot $t_k$.
success_threshold: 15% reduction in EMD compared to the WFR-based USB baseline.
failure_condition: The neural semi-coupling fails to converge or produces higher EMD than the static WFR approximation.
first_24h_experiment: Implement a 1D synthetic branching process (e.g., a bifurcating Gaussian) and compare the USB (WFR-based) marginals against the exact analytical BSB marginals.
specific_intervention: Replace the RUOT/WFR static coupling in Algorithm 2, Step 3, with a learned variational coupling specific to the BSB problem.
baseline: USB with WFR approximation (as described in the paper).
story_angle: Bridging the gap between continuous flow matching and discrete biological branching through exact mathematical couplings.
